one_liner: A 22-year-old woman presents with irregular menstrual periods, worsening facial hair, and 30 pounds of weight gain over 2 years.

stage2.hpi_extension: Her periods come every 2-4 months since menarche at age 13. She has noticed dark coarse hair on her chin, upper lip, and lower abdomen and is plucking most days. She has also developed cystic acne and dark velvety patches on her neck. She and her partner have been trying to conceive for 8 months without success. She denies galactorrhea, visual changes, or cold intolerance.
stage2.family_history: Mother with type 2 diabetes; sister with irregular periods and infertility
stage2.physical_exam: General: BMI 34 (obese). HEENT: terminal hair on chin and upper lip, cystic acne on face and back. Neck: acanthosis nigricans on the posterior neck. CV: regular. Lungs: clear. Abdomen: obese, soft, non-tender, coarse midline hair extending toward umbilicus. Pelvic: normal external genitalia, clitoris not enlarged, non-tender adnexa bilaterally.

lab_results.result: Total testosterone mildly elevated, free testosterone elevated, DHEA-S normal, LH/FSH ratio 2.8:1, prolactin normal, TSH normal, 17-hydroxyprogesterone normal (rules out non-classic CAH). Beta-hCG negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Biochemical hyperandrogenism with an elevated LH:FSH ratio greater than 2:1 and normal prolactin/TSH/17-OHP is classic for PCOS after exclusion of other causes.

lab_results.result: Transvaginal ultrasound: bilateral enlarged ovaries with greater than 12 small peripheral follicles per ovary in a 'string of pearls' distribution and increased ovarian stromal volume. Endometrium mildly thickened.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Polycystic-appearing ovaries on ultrasound fulfill one of the Rotterdam criteria. The patient meets all three (oligo-ovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries), making the diagnosis definitive.

lab_results.result: Fasting glucose 112 (impaired fasting glucose), HbA1c 6.1% (prediabetes), fasting insulin elevated, HOMA-IR elevated, total cholesterol 228, triglycerides 198, HDL 38.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and impaired glucose tolerance are core metabolic features of PCOS and substantially increase long-term risk of type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease.

stage5.final_clue: She is counseled on lifestyle modification and started on metformin plus a combined oral contraceptive for cycle regulation and hirsutism, and letrozole is planned for ovulation induction when she is ready to resume conception attempts.

debrief.classic_presentation: PCOS classically presents in reproductive-age women with the triad of menstrual irregularity (oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea from anovulation), clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne, male pattern alopecia), and polycystic-appearing ovaries on ultrasound. The Rotterdam criteria require two of these three features, after excluding other causes of androgen excess. Obesity, acanthosis nigricans, insulin resistance, infertility, and metabolic syndrome are frequent accompaniments.
debrief.pathophysiology: Insulin resistance plays a central role: hyperinsulinemia stimulates ovarian theca cells to produce excess androgens and suppresses hepatic sex hormone-binding globulin, increasing free testosterone. Abnormal GnRH pulsatility increases LH relative to FSH, producing an elevated LH:FSH ratio that further drives ovarian androgen production. Insufficient FSH stimulation prevents follicular maturation, leading to anovulation and the accumulation of arrested follicles ('string of pearls'). Unopposed estrogen from anovulation increases long-term risk of endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma.
debrief.treatment_overview: Management is guided by the patient's goals. For patients not seeking pregnancy, combined oral contraceptives regulate cycles, suppress ovarian androgens, and protect the endometrium; spironolactone can be added for hirsutism. Metformin improves insulin sensitivity and may restore ovulation. Lifestyle modification (weight loss, exercise) improves all features of PCOS. For infertility, letrozole is now first-line ovulation induction, followed by clomiphene and gonadotropins. Screen for and treat diabetes, dyslipidemia, sleep apnea, and endometrial hyperplasia.
high_yield_pearls: Rotterdam criteria: 2 of 3 of oligo-ovulation, hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound.
Elevated LH:FSH ratio (greater than 2:1) and insulin resistance are hallmark biochemical features.
Unopposed estrogen from chronic anovulation increases risk of endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma.
First-line for ovulation induction is letrozole (aromatase inhibitor); clomiphene is an alternative.
Metformin improves insulin resistance and menstrual regularity; combined OCPs treat hirsutism and cycle control.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Reproductive chapter, 'Ovarian disorders — Polycystic ovary syndrome' section.
